speaker: interviewer
text: Survivor Pola Grinstein, tape number 2. October 23, 1996. Mrs. Grinstein, I asked you before changing tapes how Hitler coming to power, gaining power in Europe-- how that affected your hometown and your home.

speaker: interviewee
text: When Hitler come to power, then from Poland-- we come before to Poland. Then they start to run away, the people.

speaker: interviewer
text: From where?

speaker: interviewee
text: From the occupation from when Hitler came. You know, he started already there, you know. In '39, you know, they were very afraid, and they ran out, many people. They called them [NON-ENGLISH], refugees. And they told what's going on there. We didn't believe.

speaker: interviewer
text: They came to your house?

speaker: interviewee
text: We had five families. On the farm, we had three families, and on the farm, we needed to take them away, because the goyim, you know, they started, you know, taking Jews. We want to make already a Jewish colony. Then my father, then we take them back to the shtetl.

speaker: interviewee
text: And nobody could believe. He couldn't believe. My father say, how could it be that, to kill people, innocent people? Couldn't be. What else did they tell, the refugees?

speaker: interviewee
text: The refugees told that they killed Jews. They take away everything from the Jews. That they cannot go to school. They fired all the people, what they work in the government, you know. They say all-- but this was unbelievable. They couldn't believe.

speaker: interviewer
text: The refugees--

speaker: interviewee
text: And then--

speaker: interviewer
text: --that your father took in--

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you remember where they came from?

speaker: interviewee
text: One come-- I remember one come from Tarnow-- Ternopil. Not Tarnow, Ternopil. One come from-- two from Radom. [? Kaiser ?] three, a family from-- married people, a family. It was a pharmacist with a wife and two children. Then was the father and the mother, retired already. Then was two sons. And there was [NON-ENGLISH], and [? Kaiser ?] was in-- I don't remember the-- anyway, they want to go back.

speaker: interviewer
text: They wanted to go back to their hometowns?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, because was so crowded, so already terrible. And my father said, if you saw what is going on, why you want to go back? Don't go back. And they didn't go back. OK, they didn't go back. They didn't want to take the citizens, the Russian citizens. And they send them to Russia.

speaker: interviewer
text: Those families?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, those families. They send them to Russia. And my father was so sorry. My God, my God, what will be from them?

speaker: interviewer
text: Before, you mentioned the Russian occupation.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: At the time that the refugees were in your town was already Russian occupation? Yes, was already Russian occupation. And then my father-- my father was afraid, also, because he was a kulak. You know what is kulak? A business bourgeoisie, and a farm is a kulak. I went to school. I signed up to school to go, you know. I finished only seventh grade. Then I should write a biography. Everyone should write who he is. What year are we talking about now?

speaker: interviewee
text: '39. '39. June was the '39. Then I wrote a biography. And what I-- I wrote-- I wrote lies, you know, that we had a house, and this, and that. And they threw me out.

speaker: interviewer
text: From school?

speaker: interviewee
text: From school, because I lied everything. They told that every child, to catch the people with the need. Then I wrote a lie, and they threw me out from school. Then my father took me to [NON-ENGLISH], to [NON-ENGLISH], and I went there to school. My sister and brother are there.

speaker: interviewer
text: Jewish people, when the Russians came, could they still practice-- practice--

speaker: interviewee
text: Religion?

speaker: interviewer
text: --freely religion?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: What changes did you see?

speaker: interviewee
text: I will  tell you something.

speaker: interviewer
text: In general.

speaker: interviewee
text: In general, I saw some. By Poland, the rich people could marry the girls when the Russians came. They didn't need no money. Was very happy for the girls which they didn't have nothing. They didn't have nothing, you know. Then it was very good for the poor people, you know.

speaker: interviewee
text: My father was very bad, because he was with the forest business. They gave him-- they gave him, first of all, high, very high income tax, taxes. And then they gave him to bring out from the forest so much, so much metal-- not wood. [NON-ENGLISH] how you say? To build, like I told you, the railroad and so on. Then they gave him a very big-- no, we couldn't you know. And he wasn't the least [INAUDIBLE]. But they came, the Germans came, and he couldn't already. But he was very happy-- The Germans came when?

speaker: interviewee
text: The Germans came in '41. He was-- at the least, they didn't have time to send him, you know?

speaker: interviewer
text: To Siberia?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK. Let me clear a little bit something here. Why were you-- did you know, why you were under Russian occupation, you-- go back at the time when you are a young girl.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: And do you know why your town was under Russian occupation?

speaker: interviewee
text: Because Hitler and Stalin made up, to take the part from Ukraine for him, and Poland for Hitler.

speaker: interviewer
text: You were told that at home?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: Where did you learn that, at home?

speaker: interviewee
text: In home. In home I learned this, yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: When the Russians came, what changes were in your town?

speaker: interviewee
text: When the Russian came--

speaker: interviewer
text: What happened where you lived?

speaker: interviewee
text: First of all, I will tell you something. It was-- they were afraid that the Germans shouldn't come. They were already with the Russians, because they know that the Russians don't kill people, you know? If somebody-- you know, they say that there is [INAUDIBLE] or something, but so innocent people, they don't still kill. And children, you know, also not. Then-- then was already better. But-- but later on, they gave jobs for Jews. For very poor Jews, they gave school. Was no anti-Semitism anymore. You couldn't tell-- a Jew to Palestine, because the Russians don't let to Palestine. You couldn't [INAUDIBLE] because then you are a nationalist. You're up in there, you know. There was already-- this was good, very good. For the rich people, it wasn't good.

speaker: interviewer
text: And you said in '41--

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: The Germans came.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: And your father, instead of going to Siberia--

speaker: interviewee
text: He was there. He didn't go. They didn't send him.

speaker: interviewer
text: What happened to your father, then?

speaker: interviewee
text: When the Germans came in, my father, you know, because he needed to go to Siberia, at the least, and he wanted to secure me and my mother and my brother. Then he put all kinds of things by the Czechs-- hide it. He didn't have time even to tell us. And when-- when the Germans came, right away, the Czechs, they didn't want nothing to do with that. We had the farm. Half belonged to the Ukrainian, to the village, and half belonged to [? colonials, ?] both, to the Germans-- to the Germans, the Ukrainians. The Ukrainians made a meeting that we need to confiscate by the Jew the farm and to take away. They called my father. He should sign over the farm. And anyway, so you know, but the Germans-- he wouldn't have then--

speaker: interviewer
text: How did he know that?

speaker: interviewee
text: He know it already, you know. When the Germans came already to us, we see, you know, that they came and-- and saw the Jews with such a hatred. Then my father know very good Russian, Ukrainian, Polish, and German very good. He had business with the Germans, and with Ukrainians, and with the Polacks. Then when the Germans came, the Germans, in the shtetl, took him as a translator. And he told, but the Ukrainians cannot tell that he is a communist, because when the Germans came, they killed the communists-- who belonged to the communists, they killed. He wasn't afraid. My God. I was-- you know, so, you know, persecuted by them. Then he signed up. He signed up. The second day this was--

speaker: interviewer
text: Signed up where?

speaker: interviewee
text: In village in the office by the [? presidat, ?] like the mayor. He signed up. OK, and he went back.

speaker: interviewer
text: The second day, they came and say, you know, can you do it, Mr. David? Who's they? Who's they?

speaker: interviewee
text: The mayor and the helpers-- secretary, I know. And you know, you forget something to sign. He went to sign something. And we came with him this time. This time we come-- I, my mother and my brother. And we came. This was before the-- need to be cut, you know? This was--

speaker: interviewer
text: The hay?

speaker: interviewee
text: Not the hay.

speaker: interviewer
text: Wheat?

speaker: interviewee
text: Wheat. The wheat, you know, was very high to cut already, yes. Then we came, you know, and we had these workers which was living on the farm-- was living on the farm around the year around. They cared the horses and the cows and the chickens and everything. Then-- they took him, and we are waiting and waiting and waiting, you know, and he didn't come. Came one, a Polish mechanic, which my father, there was a mill where they made flour, yes, for us. He was the mechanic there. And my father gave him the job. This was belonged to the [NON-ENGLISH]. He came to us. He said, look, your father, your husband is in basement locked up.

speaker: interviewer
text: At the--

speaker: interviewee
text: The mechanic came.

speaker: interviewer
text: But which basement? From the--

speaker: interviewee
text: Over there. He went to-- he went--

speaker: interviewer
text: To the office?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, he went to the office, and he saw. Maybe he told them or something. We couldn't do nothing. We say maybe they already--

speaker: interviewer
text: Who put them there?

speaker: interviewee
text: The mayor with all of them. Then-- no, the mayor not. The other. The mayor not. The mayor made only the arrangements. The arrangements they should take away everything. Then--

speaker: interviewer
text: I need to know what kind of-- Russian or German?

speaker: interviewee
text: Ukrainian.

speaker: interviewer
text: The Ukrainian.

speaker: interviewee
text: Ukrainian-- the mayor was Ukrainian, and all of them.

speaker: interviewer
text: And who gave the orders?

speaker: interviewee
text: The Ukrainian. No, the Czechs didn't want to touch nothing. This was-- belonged to the Czech colony, the part where we used to live, the house belonged at the Czech colony, you understand?

speaker: interviewer
text: And what happened when you found out that your father was--

speaker: interviewee
text: We couldn't help nothing. Then one told, go and help your father-- one. The second say, you better go hide, and then-- and I'll tell you, run back to the shtetl, because he warned that you'll be killed, because they want that you should come to be killed.

speaker: interviewer
text: So what did you do?

speaker: interviewee
text: What we did-- I did, and the [? Davids ?], and so about when was a little bit out, we heard, you know, like these-- you know-- gunshots. Gunshots. They took two more Jews was there, and they killed. My father was a very strong man. He fighted with them. How we know, I'll just tell you. And he told, please, please, my children. My daughter doesn't have a mother. Don't kill me. At least she should have a father. And when they went, I was near by the road. And then oh, I heard. And my mother also. We went in the morning. We went to the German-- you know, to the German--

speaker: interviewer
text: Headquarters?

speaker: interviewee
text: Headquarters. And we told the whole story, and they told so that we should-- tell us who, and we'll go and we'll kill them. They didn't want that they should have such a power, the Ukrainians, to take over the Germans' power. Then the rabbi, they say he's already dead. And we are afraid maybe they'll come and they kill us, you know. Then we left this. We left this.

speaker: interviewer
text: Where did you go?

speaker: interviewee
text: We come. We run out of the shtetl. We run out the shtetl. So we had everything there was-- cows, and all equipment for a farm, and you know. And everything was there-- furniture, everything.

speaker: interviewer
text: And where did you go?

speaker: interviewee
text: We go shtetl. This was in the village, in shtetl. We went to shtetl. And-- and then start, in two months, start to [NON-ENGLISH]. The SS came. Yes, and then-- we had this officer, lieutenant, which was the head of the Jews, responsible for the Jews, Fogel-- Fogel. He had--

speaker: interviewer
text: What nationality was he?

speaker: interviewee
text: He was a German. He was from the SS-- SS. He was responsible for the Judenrat, for the ghetto-- not for ghetto, for the Jews. Then before he come, they made an action. They told that everyone should come off, come in this and this, that place to work. They come, about 200 people.

speaker: interviewer
text: How did they tell you? How did they tell you?

speaker: interviewee
text: Not me. The Judenrat. They came to the Judenrat, and told we need so much and so much workers. We didn't still know what and where. Then they said, who wants to work, come out there and there. And they went out, woman and man, and they took them, and they killed them not far away in a forest-- killed out over all the 200.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you remember the name of that forest?

speaker: interviewee
text: This was by-- a small, a small-- a [NON-ENGLISH], you know. Without name.

speaker: interviewer
text: Near what?

speaker: interviewee
text: Near Ostrozec.

speaker: interviewer
text: North? North or south?

speaker: interviewee
text: I don't remember north or south. Near Ostrozec. Around Ostrozec.

speaker: interviewer
text: Were you able to stay in your home all the time? Or you were moved to another place?

speaker: interviewee
text: Until the ghetto, yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: Until the ghetto.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: When did you have to move to the ghetto?

speaker: interviewee
text: To the ghetto, I have to move when the ghetto start.

speaker: interviewer
text: When was that? What year?

speaker: interviewee
text: The ghetto was in 42nd year, '42. '42-- was summertime.

speaker: interviewer
text: And you have to move from--

speaker: interviewee
text: They gave-- they gave a small place-- a small place that, for instance, in a room was like three, four families-- more, even, in such a room. They concentrate, and they closed up.

speaker: interviewer
text: Who went with you to the ghetto?

speaker: interviewee
text: What you say, please?

speaker: interviewer
text: Who went with you?

speaker: interviewee
text: My mother and my brother and my sister.

speaker: interviewer
text: And how far was it from your house?

speaker: interviewee
text: Wasn't far, because was a [NON-ENGLISH]. Wasn't far.

speaker: interviewer
text: What part of the shtetl?

speaker: interviewee
text: What part of the shtetl? Was like-- could be not half of the shtetl. They divided half.

speaker: interviewer
text: And who told you that you have to go to a ghetto?

speaker: interviewee
text: The Germans came and gave order to the Judenrat, and the Judenrat master will do that.

speaker: interviewer
text: When the Germans came into your shtetl--

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: --where were you? Did you see them coming in?

speaker: interviewee
text: I was lucky. Whenever was an action or something, I wasn't there, you know. I was outside. And when I was coming in, then I jumped in a garden, hiding some place. They didn't see me. But if they would saw me, they would kill me. But what-- later on, the Fogel, I want to mention. You can be SS. If you want to be good, you can be good. He fell in love with one. She was from Hungary. She married a Polish teacher. He was an exhibition in Hungary, in Budapest. He went there and he fell in love with her, and he married her. And he-- they took him. He disappeared. Then he fell in love with her. And Troyanow was his name, the teacher's. She was Troyanowa. And she was the connection between Judenrat and him. And he was so good that when once came the SS to see, to kill people, then he told the Judenrat, he said, look, tomorrow must you come. You hide. Tomorrow must you come, the SS [? cars. ?] And they want to kill and to take. You hide. And I'll come. Don't worry. I'll come with two guns. And I'll start to [? bad ?] to speak about Jews. And I'll start to [? shout. ?] You should be prepared to scare them.

speaker: interviewee
text: He came out, you know, in the middle of the shtetl, and [NON-ENGLISH]. I'll kill you. I'll kill you. And he start to shoot. The SS saw that he's so bad. He's such a momzer that they can depend on him. And they didn't come anymore. You understand? That's Fogel?

speaker: interviewee
text: Fogel. And then was Schultz. His replacement was also very good man, Schultz. Then-- but the police was Ukrainian. They was terrible. They used to beat up and to kill. They was terrible. They didn't--

speaker: interviewer
text: What was the name of the ghetto?

speaker: interviewee
text: Ostrozec. They brought-- yes, they brought in ghetto from another shtetl, [? Trovitz ?], to us, even.

speaker: interviewer
text: And they gave half-- the shtetl divided, and they put in another shtetl You understand? Such [INAUDIBLE] was already. Anyway-- How many people would you say were in that ghetto?

speaker: interviewee
text: About 2,000. Even more, because later on, because our ghetto was the last to kill. Then from Lutsk start to come, to run out.

speaker: interviewer
text: How old were you when you had to go into the ghetto?

speaker: interviewee
text: '25, How old I was, '42?-- make 14 years old. In '42 you were 14 years old. What would a 14-year-old girl do the whole day there? Tell me about a routine day in the ghetto. What is to do? We used to-- nothing. We were waiting to be killed. What is to do? Nothing. Sometimes I took a book to forget, but I couldn't concentrate myself. I couldn't. You know, any day maybe they'll come. And then-- then come-- start to come the potatoes to take out, and other work. Then they come to take workers from the ghetto. They were happy to go out a little bit from the ghetto, to be free a little bit, to eat something. Then they held-- I told you, the [NON-ENGLISH] was a countess [NON-ENGLISH]. [NON-ENGLISH] made business with my father. And she know it very well. But she disappeared when the Russians come, or maybe they arrested. I don't know exactly what was with her. But the manager [INAUDIBLE], I don't remember his name. He came to the ghetto to ask for workers. But he say, I would like workers, but I want David's, which was killed. I know, the children, the family. This was a big, big favor to take out from the ghetto people to work. It means already that you can run out to save yourself. Then--

speaker: interviewer
text: Let me ask you something. What happened to your father?

speaker: interviewee
text: My father-- I told you, he was killed.

speaker: interviewer
text: At the basement?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. They took him out from the basement in a wagon, and they killed him, and they took him to the cemetery where horses and dogs were buried. He and two more Jews.

speaker: interviewer
text: Were you told who killed him?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. I didn't know who killed him. But I'll tell you after the war what I did. I'll tell you. I took-- I took this revenge.

speaker: interviewer
text: So your father died, and you were sure about it at that time?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, of course.

speaker: interviewer
text: You knew about it. Tell me a little bit more about the ghetto. Who ran the ghetto?

speaker: interviewee
text: The Judenrat.

speaker: interviewer
text: Can you tell me-- explain to me what--

speaker: interviewee
text: I will tell you something. Our Judenrat was very good. Tried their best. But sometimes they needed something to do, you know? Then they didn't have very difficult, because it was the Fogel, you know? But later on, when came already the liquidation of the ghetto, he took two girls, a sister is in Detroit, to her sisters. They used to cook for him and her. He hided them on their--

speaker: interviewer
text: Attic?

speaker: interviewee
text: Attic. And he gave them to eat. That was a translator, a Polack. He saw this, and he told him. He told him. He was afraid. Or maybe he told the Gestapo. I don't know. And you know, and he-- and he-- they took out them and they killed them. He couldn't say nothing.

speaker: interviewer
text: We are going to change tapes now.

speaker: interviewee
text: Now I go--
